items.myth: Quicksort is always faster than mergesort.
items.reality: Quicksort is *usually* faster on random in-memory data because of cache behavior, but its worst case is O(n²) and it isn't stable. Mergesort and Timsort guarantee O(n log n) and are stable.
items.example:
```python
# Adversarial input -> Quicksort O(n²); Timsort/Mergesort still O(n log n).
```

items.myth: All comparison sorts have the same complexity.
items.reality: All comparison sorts are bounded below by Ω(n log n) in the worst case, but their best cases (and constants) differ widely. Timsort is O(n) on already-sorted input; insertion sort is too — bubble sort isn't worth the byte-cycles even though it's also bounded by Ω(n log n) below.
items.example:
```python
sorted([1,2,3,...,10_000])   # Timsort detects the run; near-O(n)
```

items.myth: Sorting is O(n log n) — no exceptions.
items.reality: Comparison sorting is Ω(n log n). Non-comparison sorts (counting, radix) can be O(n + k) when keys have small alphabets or fixed-width representations.
items.example:
```python
# Counting sort of 1M integers in [0..255] is O(n + 256) — effectively O(n).
```

items.myth: Python's `sorted` builds a new list AND mutates the original.
items.reality: `sorted(it)` returns a new list and leaves the original unchanged; `list.sort()` mutates and returns `None`.

items.myth: Random shuffling protects quicksort from its worst case entirely.
items.reality: Random pivoting makes the worst case astronomically unlikely on unrelated workloads, but adversarial inputs (or correlated keys) can still trigger it. For hard real-time guarantees prefer heapsort or mergesort.
items.example:
```python
# Worst-case-bounded:
from heapq import heapify, heappop
heapify(xs)
# pop n times for heapsort-like behavior
```
